title: Anthopic did a thing...
summary: Matthew Berman discusses a recent development or announcement from Anthropic. The video title is vague and the content text provides only promotional links without detailing what announcement is being covered.
writeup: A live-stream walkthrough of Anthropic's essay "When AI Builds Itself," which traces four historical stages of AI development abstraction (direct coding, chatbots, coding agents, autonomous multi-agent swarms) and argues that closing the loop by having agents train successor models would make compute the sole remaining bottleneck. The presenter grounds the discussion in Leopold Aschenbrenner's Situational Awareness intelligence-explosion graph and notes Anthropic's internal data showing agent task-completion horizon doubling every four months, accelerating from a prior rate of every seven months. The through-line is that recursive self-improvement is not yet present but its precursors are already measurable inside Anthropic, and that the one still-missing ingredient separates early hints from full RSI.
key_points: Anthropic's paper states they are already delegating a growing share of AI development to AI systems, with agents completing tasks whose reliable horizon doubles every four months, accelerating from the earlier doubling rate of every seven months.

The four-stage abstraction diagram in the paper maps the progression: human writes code directly (pre-2023), human talks to chatbot that builds Claude (ChatGPT era), human prompts a coding agent that delegates to sub-agents (2025-2026), and finally agents capable of building and training models themselves with no human in the loop.

Closing the loop means future Claude versions could be continuously improved by Claude itself, at which point compute quantity and parallelization become the only remaining bottleneck, with no human research or coding decisions gating progress.

Aschenbrenner's Situational Awareness paper charted an "automated Alec Radford" threshold beyond which an intelligence explosion occurs because models are no longer bottlenecked by human research bandwidth, and Anthropic's essay implicitly confirms that trajectory is on schedule.

Anthropic explicitly states that full recursive self-improvement is not yet achieved and that one ingredient is still missing, while simultaneously acknowledging that full RSI could increase the risk of humans losing control over AI systems.
